title: Textual Variants Explained
subtitle: Why Bible manuscripts differ, what Mark 16 and John 8 have to do with it, and how the Quran's single consonantal text compares
summary: Textual criticism is the respected scholarly practice — largely developed and practiced by Christian scholars themselves — of comparing ancient handwritten Bible manuscripts to recover the earliest wording. Most of the many thousands of differences found this way are trivial spelling or word-order changes, but a few, like the longer ending of Mark and the story of the woman caught in adultery in John, are openly discussed by scholars in footnotes of nearly every modern Bible. This article walks through those two well-known cases in plain language, then compares that history of manuscript divergence with the Quran's single Uthmanic consonantal text, which did not develop comparable rival textual families.

seo.metaTitle: Textual Variants Explained: Bible Manuscripts vs. the Quran's Text
seo.metaDescription: A beginner's guide to New Testament textual criticism — including Mark 16:9-20 and John 7:53-8:11 — and how it compares to the Quran's Uthmanic consonantal text.

sections.title: In Short
sections.body:
Textual criticism is the scholarly work of comparing ancient handwritten copies of a text — copies made before printing existed — in order to reconstruct, as closely as possible, the earliest recoverable wording. Applied to the New Testament, this field has catalogued more than five thousand Greek manuscripts and tens of thousands of small differences between them, called textual variants.

The overwhelming majority of these variants are inconsequential: a misspelled word, a different word order, an article added or dropped. A very small number are substantial enough that mainstream Christian scholarship discusses them openly, including the last twelve verses of the Gospel of Mark and the story of the woman caught in adultery in the Gospel of John.

This article explains what those two well-known cases are, why respected Christian scholars themselves flag them in modern study Bibles rather than hide them, and how this whole picture compares with the way the Quran's Arabic consonantal text was standardized within a generation of Muhammad's death, without producing rival textual families of the same kind.

sections.title: What Is a Textual Variant?
sections.body:
Before printing presses existed, every copy of every book had to be written out by hand. Whenever a scribe copied a manuscript, no matter how careful, small differences crept in — a skipped line, a word spelled differently, two similar-sounding words swapped. Multiply that across many centuries and many copies, and you end up with thousands of manuscripts that agree on almost everything but differ from each other in thousands of small places. Scholars call each of these differences a textual variant.

Studying these variants is called textual criticism, and it is one of the oldest and most respected disciplines in biblical scholarship. It is not a modern attack on faith. Devout Christian scholars have practiced it for centuries — from the Renaissance humanist Erasmus preparing the first printed Greek New Testament, to twentieth-century scholars like Bruce Metzger, to the seminary professors who teach it today. Their goal has always been the same: to get as close as possible to what the biblical authors actually wrote, by comparing the many surviving copies against one another.

This article looks at two of the best-known textual variants — the ending of Mark and the story of the woman caught in adultery in John — and explains why mainstream scholarship discusses them so openly. It then compares that picture with the history of the Quran's text, fixed into a single standardized consonantal form within about twenty years of Muhammad's death.

sections.title: The Question This Article Answers
sections.body:
Both the Bible and the Quran were transmitted for a period by hand-copying before the age of printing. So a fair question is this: why does the New Testament have thousands of surviving manuscripts sorted by scholars into recognizably different textual families, with tens of thousands of small variants between them and a handful of well-known larger ones, while the Quran's Arabic consonantal text does not show a comparable pattern of rival textual families?

Answering that question honestly means taking each side's history on its own terms first, without exaggerating either one. It does not mean claiming the New Testament is unreliable, and it does not mean glossing over the fact that Quranic recitation itself contains a small number of sanctioned reading differences, called qiraat. It means looking carefully at what kind of variation each tradition actually produced, and why.

sections.title: Two Different Transmission Histories
sections.body:
The New Testament was copied by hand for roughly 1,400 years before the printing press reached Europe in the 1450s, across a vast area — Egypt, Palestine, Syria, Asia Minor, Greece, Italy, North Africa — with no central authority checking each new copy against a master original. Today more than 5,700 Greek manuscripts of the New Testament survive, along with thousands more in Latin, Coptic, Syriac, and other languages, plus quotations in early Christian writers. Comparing all of them turns up tens of thousands of textual variants. Scholars have long grouped these manuscripts into recognizable regional families, commonly called the Alexandrian, Western, Byzantine, and Caesarean text-types, reflecting how the text drifted somewhat differently in different regions before anyone attempted a unified, empire-wide standardization of the wording.

The Quran's transmission history took a different shape. According to Islamic historical accounts, verses were both memorized and written down on available materials by scribes during Muhammad's own lifetime, so writing and memorization ran side by side from the start rather than writing catching up to an already-diverging oral tradition. After Muhammad's death in 632 CE, the first caliph, Abu Bakr, had the material gathered into a single collected text. Within about two decades of that, the third caliph, Uthman, addressed growing regional differences in recitation by commissioning an authoritative copy based on Zayd ibn Thabit's compilation, sending copies of this single consonantal skeleton (called the rasm) to the main garrison cities of the growing Muslim world, and having competing partial copies set aside — a centralized, one-time standardization rather than a centuries-long, decentralized copying process. Consistent with that account, a Quran manuscript held at the University of Birmingham, radiocarbon-dated by the University of Oxford to somewhere between AD 568 and 645, has been described by the university as some of the earliest physical textual evidence of the Quran known to survive, consistent with a text that has undergone little alteration since close to Muhammad's own era.

sections.title: The Quran's Own Claim About Its Preservation
sections.body:
The Quran makes an explicit claim about its own preservation. In Surah Al-Hijr, God says: "إِنَّا نَحْنُ نَزَّلْنَا ٱلذِّكْرَ وَإِنَّا لَهُۥ لَحَٰفِظُونَ" — "Indeed, it is We who sent down the Qur'an and indeed, We will be its guardian" (Quran 15:9).

Muslims understand this verse as God's promise to protect the core revealed text of the Quran from being lost or corrupted over time. Historically, that promise is understood to have been worked out through the process described above: verses recorded in writing during Muhammad's lifetime, an unbroken chain of memorizers (huffaz) reciting the whole text from the beginning, and then Uthman's swift standardization of a single consonantal skeleton distributed to the major cities of the Muslim world, foreclosing the kind of centuries-long, uncoordinated regional drift that produced the New Testament's many textual families.

This does not mean the Quran's transmission has no internal variation at all. A small number of differences in vowelling, pronunciation, and minor word forms, called qiraat, were preserved as multiple authenticated oral traditions going back to the Prophet, all fitting within Uthman's single consonantal skeleton. These are recognized, bounded variant readings passed down through certified chains of transmission — not independent manuscript lineages that diverged silently over time the way New Testament text-types did.

sections.title: Mark's Ending and the Woman Caught in Adultery
sections.body:
The two most widely discussed textual variants in the New Testament both involve substantial passages, not single words, which is exactly why they are so well known and so openly discussed.

The first is the ending of Mark's Gospel. Most printed Bibles include Mark 16:9-20, which reads in part: "Now when he had risen early on the first day of the week, he appeared first to Mary Magdalene, from whom he had cast out seven demons... He said to them, 'Go into all the world, and preach the Good News to the whole creation. He who believes and is baptized will be saved; but he who disbelieves will be condemned'... So then the Lord, after he had spoken to them, was received up into heaven, and sat down at the right hand of God" (Mark 16:9, 15-16, 19). The two oldest and most highly regarded complete Greek manuscripts of the New Testament, Codex Sinaiticus and Codex Vaticanus, both copied in the fourth century, end Mark's Gospel at verse 8 instead, without these final twelve verses. Because of this and other manuscript evidence, most modern textual scholars conclude that verses 9-20 were added sometime after Mark's Gospel first circulated, even though the passage is ancient and was already known by some Christian writers within a century or two of the New Testament period.

The second is the story in John's Gospel of a woman brought to Jesus after being caught in adultery. It reads, in the World English Bible: "Everyone went to his own house, but Jesus went to the Mount of Olives... The scribes and the Pharisees brought a woman taken in adultery... 'Teacher, we found this woman in adultery, in the very act. Now in our law, Moses commanded us to stone such women. What then do you say about her?'... 'He who is without sin among you, let him throw the first stone at her'... 'Woman, where are your accusers? Did no one condemn you?' She said, 'No one, Lord.' Jesus said, 'Neither do I condemn you. Go your way. From now on, sin no more'" (John 7:53; 8:3-11). This beloved passage is likewise missing from the earliest surviving Greek manuscripts of John, and in some later manuscripts that include it, it appears in a different location entirely, sometimes even attached to Luke's Gospel. Mainstream biblical scholarship, including scholarship from within Christian seminaries, generally treats it as an early and cherished story about Jesus that circulated separately before being inserted into John's Gospel here, rather than as part of John's original text.

What matters here is not the specific verdict on either passage, but the transparency involved in reaching it. Nearly every modern study Bible — Protestant and Catholic alike — flags both passages with brackets or footnotes explaining the manuscript evidence, so ordinary readers can see exactly what the textual question is. That kind of open acknowledgment is a mark of intellectual honesty within Christian scholarship, not evidence of concealment.

sections.title: Comparing the Two Transmission Patterns
sections.body:
Laid side by side, the two histories differ in kind, not just in degree. The New Testament's text passed through roughly fourteen centuries of hand-copying across a wide, religiously decentralized geography before anyone printed a fixed edition. No single authority ever issued one master copy and asked everyone to destroy competing versions. The natural result, as thousands of surviving manuscripts show, was the gradual emergence of recognizable regional textual families and tens of thousands of accumulated variants, a small number of which — like Mark's ending and the story in John 7:53-8:11 — involve entire paragraphs rather than single words.

The Quran's text, by contrast, was fixed by a one-time, top-down recension within about two decades of Muhammad's death, while companions who had memorized the entire revelation were still alive to check the written copies against living memory. Competing personal codices were collected and set aside rather than left to multiply independently for centuries. This does not mean the Quran's transmission has been free of any variation whatsoever — the sanctioned qiraat readings show it has not — but those differences are certified oral variants within a single agreed consonantal skeleton, not independently evolving manuscript lineages of the kind that produced Mark's alternate ending or John's floating story.

sections.title: Common Misunderstandings
sections.body:
A first misunderstanding is that the sheer number of New Testament variants — often cited as being in the tens of thousands — means the text is hopelessly corrupted. Mainstream scholarship, including the work of Christian textual critics, points out that the great majority are exactly what you would expect from centuries of hand-copying: spelling differences, word-order changes, and obvious slips of the pen, none of which affect meaning. Only a small number, like the two discussed here, involve larger stretches of text.

A second misunderstanding runs the other way: assuming Islamic tradition claims the Quran was copied for fourteen centuries with absolutely zero human variation of any kind. In reality, the qiraat readings are a recognized, if narrower, form of textual variation, preserved through certified oral chains rather than suppressed. The relevant contrast is not "zero variation versus enormous variation," but a bounded, centrally managed, orally certified variation versus an open-ended, multi-century, geographically scattered manuscript divergence.

A third misunderstanding is that textual criticism itself is a hostile, secular project aimed at undermining Christianity. In fact, it has been practiced for centuries mostly by devout Christian scholars working inside the church's own scholarly tradition, precisely because they care about recovering the most accurate possible text of Scripture. Flagging passages like Mark 16:9-20 or John 7:53-8:11 in a footnote is the discipline doing its job well, not evidence against the faith of the scholars doing it.

sections.title: What This Comparison Suggests
sections.body:
None of this is offered to mock the New Testament or the Christian scholars who study it with such care and honesty; their transparency about difficult manuscript questions is, if anything, admirable. But the comparison is instructive. The Quran's claim in Surah Al-Hijr — "Indeed, it is We who sent down the Qur'an and indeed, We will be its guardian" (Quran 15:9) — is a claim that can be tested against history, and the historical record of a swift, centralized recension producing a single stable consonantal text, supported by early physical evidence like the Birmingham folios radiocarbon-dated to the first century of Islam, fits that claim well.

The New Testament's history, honestly described by its own best scholars, is one of long, decentralized, hand-copied transmission that produced real and substantial textual questions — not because anyone acted in bad faith, but because that is what happens to any text copied by hand across many regions for over a thousand years without a unifying, one-time standardization. For a sincere seeker comparing the two scriptures, that difference in transmission history is one honest piece of evidence worth weighing.

sections.title: Frequently Asked Questions
sections.body:
Q: Does the existence of thousands of New Testament variants mean the Bible cannot be trusted at all?
A: Mainstream textual scholars, many of them devout Christians, note that the vast majority of variants are trivial spelling or word-order differences that do not change meaning. Only a small number of passages, like the two discussed here, involve larger questions, and those are openly footnoted rather than hidden.

Q: Are Mark 16:9-20 and John 7:53-8:11 removed from modern Bibles?
A: No. Almost all modern translations still print both passages in full, usually with brackets or a footnote explaining that the earliest manuscripts lack them.

Q: Does the Quran have any variant readings at all?
A: Yes. The qiraat are certified oral reading traditions affecting vowelling and some word forms, all consistent with the single consonantal skeleton fixed under Caliph Uthman — a bounded, documented variation, not an uncontrolled multi-family divergence like the New Testament's.

Q: Is textual criticism an attack on Christianity?
A: No. It is a long-established discipline practiced mostly by Christian scholars themselves, aimed at recovering the earliest recoverable New Testament text through careful comparison of manuscripts.

Q: Why did Uthman have some Quran copies set aside?
A: According to Islamic historical accounts, Uthman acted to prevent regional divergence in recitation, standardizing on a single consonantal text sent to the main cities of the Muslim world, based on the compilation prepared earlier under Abu Bakr.

sections.title: Further Reading
sections.body: The following works, written from a range of perspectives, allow readers to go deeper into New Testament textual criticism and the history of the Quran's compilation and standardization.

quranVerses.arabic: إِنَّا نَحْنُ نَزَّلْنَا ٱلذِّكْرَ وَإِنَّا لَهُۥ لَحَٰفِظُونَ
quranVerses.translation: Indeed, it is We who sent down the Qur'an and indeed, We will be its guardian.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

bibleVerses.text: Now when he had risen early on the first day of the week, he appeared first to Mary Magdalene, from whom he had cast out seven demons. She went and told those who had been with him, as they mourned and wept. When they heard that he was alive, and had been seen by her, they disbelieved. After these things he was revealed in another form to two of them, as they walked, on their way into the country. They went away and told it to the rest. They didn’t believe them, either. Afterward he was revealed to the eleven themselves as they sat at the table, and he rebuked them for their unbelief and hardness of heart, because they didn’t believe those who had seen him after he had risen. He said to them, “Go into all the world, and preach the Good News to the whole creation. He who believes and is baptized will be saved; but he who disbelieves will be condemned. These signs will accompany those who believe: in my name they will cast out demons; they will speak with new languages; they will take up serpents; and if they drink any deadly thing, it will in no way hurt them; they will lay hands on the sick, and they will recover.” So then the Lord, after he had spoken to them, was received up into heaven, and sat down at the right hand of God. They went out, and preached everywhere, the Lord working with them, and confirming the word by the signs that followed. Amen.
Everyone went to his own house,

but Jesus went to the Mount of Olives. Now very early in the morning, he came again into the temple, and all the people came to him. He sat down, and taught them. The scribes and the Pharisees brought a woman taken in adultery. Having set her in the middle, they told him, “Teacher, we found this woman in adultery, in the very act. Now in our law, Moses commanded us to stone such women. What then do you say about her?” They said this testing him, that they might have something to accuse him of. But Jesus stooped down, and wrote on the ground with his finger. But when they continued asking him, he looked up and said to them, “He who is without sin among you, let him throw the first stone at her.” Again he stooped down, and with his finger wrote on the ground. They, when they heard it, being convicted by their conscience, went out one by one, beginning from the oldest, even to the last. Jesus was left alone with the woman where she was, in the middle. Jesus, standing up, saw her and said, “Woman, where are your accusers? Did no one condemn you?” She said, “No one, Lord.” Jesus said, “Neither do I condemn you. Go your way. From now on, sin no more.”

faq.question: Does the existence of thousands of New Testament variants mean the Bible cannot be trusted at all?
faq.answer: Mainstream textual scholars, many of them devout Christians, consistently note that the vast majority of variants are trivial spelling or word-order differences that do not change meaning. Only a small number of passages involve larger questions, and those are openly footnoted rather than hidden.

faq.question: Are Mark 16:9-20 and John 7:53-8:11 removed from modern Bibles?
faq.answer: No. Almost all modern translations still print both passages in full, usually with brackets or a footnote explaining that the earliest manuscripts lack them, so readers can see exactly what the textual evidence shows.

faq.question: Does the Quran have any variant readings at all?
faq.answer: Yes. The qiraat are a small number of certified oral reading traditions affecting vowelling and some word forms, all consistent with the single consonantal skeleton fixed under Caliph Uthman — a bounded, documented variation, not an uncontrolled multi-family divergence.

faq.question: Is textual criticism an attack on Christianity?
faq.answer: No. It is a long-established scholarly discipline practiced mostly by Christian scholars themselves, aimed at recovering the earliest recoverable New Testament text through careful comparison of manuscripts.

faq.question: Why did Uthman have some Quran copies set aside?
faq.answer: According to Islamic historical accounts, Uthman acted to prevent the kind of regional divergence in recitation that was beginning to appear, standardizing on a single consonantal text sent to the main cities of the Muslim world, based on the compilation prepared earlier under Abu Bakr.

furtherReading.title: The Text of the New Testament: Its Transmission, Corruption, and Restoration
furtherReading.author: Bruce M. Metzger and Bart D. Ehrman
furtherReading.note: A standard, widely cited textbook on New Testament textual criticism, covering manuscript families and famous variant passages.

furtherReading.title: Misquoting Jesus: The Story Behind Who Changed the Bible and Why
furtherReading.author: Bart D. Ehrman
furtherReading.note: A popular-level introduction to New Testament textual variants, including the ending of Mark and the story in John 7:53-8:11.

furtherReading.title: The New Testament Documents: Are They Reliable?
furtherReading.author: F. F. Bruce
furtherReading.note: A classic evangelical Christian defense of the New Testament's manuscript reliability, written by a respected biblical scholar.

furtherReading.title: The History of the Qur'anic Text: From Revelation to Compilation
furtherReading.author: Muhammad Mustafa Al-Azami
furtherReading.note: A detailed Muslim scholarly account of the Quran's compilation and the Uthmanic standardization, written partly in response to Western textual criticism of the Quran.
